name: Israeli Shakshuka
steps: Simmer the tomatoes and onion with the turmeric for 10-12 minutes until thickened.
Make small wells in the sauce and crack the eggs into them.
Cover and cook 5-6 minutes until the eggs are set to your liking.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, garlic, and jalapeno if using in the oil until softened, 5-6 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes and turmeric, and simmer 12-15 minutes until thickened into a rich sauce.
Make small wells in the sauce and crack the eggs into them.
Cover and cook 5-8 minutes until the whites are set but the yolks are still soft, or longer if you prefer them firm.
Shakshuka is one of Israel's most beloved breakfast dishes, eaten with warm bread for scooping.
description: Shakshuka, eggs poached in a spiced tomato sauce, shared across the wider Middle East and North Africa, now closely associated with Israeli breakfast.

name: Israeli Salad

ingredients.note: cup, chopped, as a parsley stand-in

steps: Toss the finely diced cucumber, tomatoes, and onion with the lime juice, oil, and herbs.
Season with salt and serve fresh - Israeli salad, chopped very fine, is a breakfast fixture, often served alongside eggs and bread.
steps_detailed: Toss the finely diced cucumber, tomatoes, and onion with the lime juice, oil, and herbs.
Season with salt and serve fresh - Israeli salad, chopped very fine, is a breakfast fixture, often served alongside eggs and bread.
Real fresh parsley is used here now rather than cilantro standing in for it - the two herbs taste meaningfully different, and this dish specifically calls for parsley's flavor.
description: A finely diced cucumber-tomato salad, a genuine Israeli breakfast staple.

name: Israeli Shakshuka-Style Green Version
steps: Saute the greens and garlic until wilted, about 5 minutes.
Make wells and crack in the eggs, then top with the feta.
Cover and cook 5-6 minutes until the eggs are set.
steps_detailed: Saute the greens and garlic in the oil until wilted and tender, 5-7 minutes.
Make small wells in the greens and crack in the eggs, scattering the feta over the top.
Cover and cook 5-8 minutes until the eggs are set to your liking - a green take on shakshuka, popular in Israeli home cooking.
description: A green variation on shakshuka, built on herbs and greens instead of tomato.

name: Israeli Sabich
steps: Fry the eggplant slices in the oil until golden, about 3-4 minutes per side.
Layer the eggplant and sliced egg in the bread.
Serve with tahini sauce if you have it.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps_detailed: Fry the eggplant slices in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until deeply golden and soft.
Whisk the tahini with the lime juice and a splash of water into a smooth sauce.
Layer the fried eggplant and sliced hard-boiled egg in the bread, drizzling with the tahini sauce - sabich, brought by Iraqi Jewish immigrants, has become a distinctly Israeli sandwich.
description: Sabich, fried eggplant and hard-boiled egg in pita, an Iraqi-Jewish dish that became an Israeli street food staple.

name: Israeli Chicken Schnitzel
steps: Bread the chicken (flour, then egg) and fry until golden and cooked through, 3-4 minutes per side.
Serve with a simple salad.
steps_detailed: Pound the chicken to an even thickness and season with salt and pepper.
Dredge in flour, then egg, then the cornmeal.
Fry in the oil over medium-high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden and cooked through.
Schnitzel is one of the most popular everyday meals in Israel, a legacy of Central European Jewish immigration.
description: Schnitzel, brought by Central European Jewish immigrants, became one of Israel's most common everyday dinners.

name: Israeli Hummus with Pearl Couscous

ingredients.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids
Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)
Bob's Red Mill Traditional Pearl Couscous

steps: Blend the chickpeas, tahini, and lime juice into hummus.
Cook the couscous and top the hummus with it.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids
Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)
Bob's Red Mill Traditional Pearl Couscous

steps_detailed: Blend the chickpeas, tahini, lime juice, and garlic into a smooth hummus, thinning with a little water as needed.
Toast the dry pearl couscous briefly in the oil, then cook in water according to the package until tender, 8-10 minutes.
Top the hummus with the warm pearl couscous.
description: Ptitim, invented in Israel in the 1950s and nicknamed 'Israeli couscous' for its shape, is properly a small toasted pasta pearl - real pearl couscous is a much closer match in both size and toasted flavor than egg noodles, which don't capture either.

name: Israeli Kubbeh Soup
steps: Soak the bulgur, then mix with a little of the beef to form small dumplings.
Brown the remaining beef, add the broth and tomatoes, and simmer 20-25 minutes.
Add the dumplings and simmer 8-10 more minutes.
steps_detailed: Soak the bulgur in hot water 15 minutes, then drain and mix with a small portion of the beef to form a dough, shaping into small dumplings.
Brown the remaining beef with the onion, then add the beef broth, diced tomatoes, and lime juice, and simmer 20-28 minutes.
Gently add the dumplings and simmer 8-12 more minutes until they're cooked through.
Kubbeh soup, brought by Iraqi and Kurdish Jewish immigrants, is a beloved Israeli comfort food.
description: Kubbeh, semolina or bulgur dumplings in broth, an Iraqi-Jewish dish now common in Israeli cooking.

name: Israeli Chicken Shawarma
steps: Marinate the chicken with the turmeric, garlic, and lime juice for at least 20 minutes.
Pan-fry over high heat, 5-6 minutes, until well browned and cooked through.
Serve with flatbread, hummus, and Israeli salad.
steps_detailed: Marinate the thinly sliced chicken with the turmeric, garlic, lime juice, nutmeg, and oil for at least 30 minutes.
Cook over high heat in a wide skillet, 6-8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until deeply browned and crisp at the edges.
Serve with warm flatbread, hummus, and Israeli salad - shawarma is ubiquitous street food across Israel.
description: Shawarma, meat traditionally stacked and roasted on a vertical spit, a beloved Israeli street food.

name: Israeli Shakshuka with Merguez-Style Sausage

ingredients.note: 1.5 servings, sliced, as a merguez stand-in

steps: Brown the sausage with the onion.
Add the tomatoes and simmer 10-12 minutes.
Make wells, crack in the eggs, cover, and cook 5-6 minutes.

ingredients_full.note: 1.75 servings, sliced, as a merguez stand-in

steps_detailed: Brown the sliced sausage with the onion, garlic, and jalapeno, 6-8 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes and simmer 12-15 minutes until thickened.
Make wells in the sauce, crack in the eggs, cover, and cook 5-8 minutes until set to your liking - a heartier, spicier shakshuka variation with North African Jewish roots.
description: Shakshuka given extra heartiness with spiced sausage.

name: Israeli Beef and Eggplant Stew

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Add the eggplant and tomatoes, and simmer, covered, 1.25-1.5 hours until the beef is tender.
Serve with rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion, garlic, and cinnamon over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the eggplant and diced tomatoes.
Cover and simmer over low heat 1.25-1.75 hours until the beef is completely tender and the eggplant has broken down into the sauce.
Serve with rice - a hearty stew reflecting Israel's blend of Middle Eastern and Mediterranean cooking traditions.
description: A simple beef and eggplant stew.

name: Israeli Cholent

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Combine the beef, beans, potato, and barley in a pot with water to cover.
Cover tightly and simmer on very low heat for 2-3 hours, or as long as you can, until everything is deeply tender.
Serve hot.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion, then add the beans, potato, barley, and sugar with enough water to cover everything generously.

Bring to a bare simmer, cover tightly, and cook on the lowest possible heat for 3-5 hours (traditionally cholent cooks overnight, since observant Jewish households don't cook on the Sabbath itself), until deeply tender and the flavors have melded completely.
Serve hot - cholent is the classic Sabbath stew, designed to be started before sundown Friday and eaten Saturday.
description: Cholent, a slow-cooked Sabbath stew traditionally left to cook overnight since cooking isn't permitted on the Sabbath itself.

name: Israeli Falafel

ingredients.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids

ingredients.note: cup, chopped, as a parsley stand-in

steps: Blend the chickpeas, garlic, herbs, flour, and turmeric in a food processor into a coarse, moldable paste - avoid over-blending into a smooth puree.
Shape into small balls or patties.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until deeply golden and crisp outside, tender within.
Falafel is one of the most iconic and widely eaten street foods in Israel.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids

steps_detailed: Blend the chickpeas, garlic, herbs, flour, and turmeric in a food processor into a coarse, moldable paste - avoid over-blending into a smooth puree.
Shape into small balls or patties.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until deeply golden and crisp outside, tender within.
Falafel is one of the most iconic and widely eaten street foods in Israel.
Real fresh parsley is used here now rather than cilantro standing in for it - the two herbs taste meaningfully different, and this dish specifically calls for parsley's flavor.
description: Falafel, fried chickpea patties, one of Israel's most iconic street foods.

name: Israeli Bourekas
steps: Layer the phyllo with the melted butter, then fill with the feta and fold into small packets.
Brush with the egg wash.
Bake at 375F for 20-25 minutes until deeply golden.
steps_detailed: Layer sheets of the phyllo, brushing each with melted butter, then fill with the crumbled feta and fold into small triangular or square packets, sealing well.
Brush the tops with the egg wash and scatter with sesame seeds.
Bake at 375F (190C) for 22-28 minutes until deeply golden and flaky - bourekas are a beloved Israeli bakery staple, filled countless ways.
description: Bourekas, filled phyllo pastries, brought by Sephardic and Balkan Jewish communities.

name: Israeli Roasted Eggplant with Tahini

ingredients.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

ingredients.note: cup, chopped, as a parsley stand-in

steps: Brush the eggplant slices with the oil and roast at 425F (220C) for 22-28 minutes until soft and lightly charred at the edges.
Whisk the tahini with the lime juice and a splash of water into a smooth, pourable sauce.
Drizzle over the roasted eggplant and scatter with herbs - a simple, popular Israeli side or snack.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps_detailed: Brush the eggplant slices with the oil and roast at 425F (220C) for 22-28 minutes until soft and lightly charred at the edges.
Whisk the tahini with the lime juice and a splash of water into a smooth, pourable sauce.
Drizzle over the roasted eggplant and scatter with herbs - a simple, popular Israeli side or snack.
Real fresh parsley is used here now rather than cilantro standing in for it - the two herbs taste meaningfully different, and this dish specifically calls for parsley's flavor.
description: Roasted eggplant with tahini, a simple, common Israeli side.

name: Israeli Matbucha
steps: Saute the bell pepper and garlic in the oil until softened.
Add the tomatoes and simmer 20-25 minutes until thickened into a rich, jammy dip.
Serve with bread.
steps_detailed: Saute the bell pepper, garlic, and jalapeno in the oil over medium heat, 6-8 minutes, until softened.
Add the tomatoes and simmer 22-30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until thickened into a rich, jammy dip.
Serve warm or at room temperature with bread - matbucha, of North African Jewish origin, is now a fixture of Israeli mezze.
description: Matbucha, a cooked tomato and pepper relish, a North African-Jewish dish now common across Israel.

name: Israeli Malabi
steps: Whisk the milk, sugar, and cornstarch together in a saucepan.
Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, 5-6 minutes until thickened.
Stir in the rosewater and chill before serving.
steps_detailed: Whisk the milk, sugar, and cornstarch together in a saucepan until no lumps remain.
Cook over medium heat, whisking constantly, 6-8 minutes until thick and pudding-like.
Stir in the rosewater off the heat, then pour into serving cups.
Chill at least 2 hours, then top with chopped pistachios before serving - malabi, a milk pudding, is a beloved Israeli dessert with roots across the wider Levant and Ottoman world.
description: Malabi, a rosewater-scented milk pudding, a beloved Israeli dessert with roots across the wider Middle East.

name: Israeli Date Cookies
steps: Cream the butter and sugar, then mix in the flour to form a soft dough.
Fold in the chopped dates.
Shape into small rounds and bake at 350F for 14-18 minutes until golden.
steps_detailed: Cream the butter and sugar together until light, then beat in the vanilla.
Mix in the flour until a soft dough forms, then fold in the chopped dates.
Shape into small rounds and bake at 350F (175C) for 15-20 minutes until golden - a simple, sweet Israeli cookie.
description: Simple date-filled cookies, similar in spirit to ma'amoul.

name: Israeli Sufganiyot
steps: Mix the flour, egg, and a little warm milk into a soft dough. Rest 20 minutes.
Shape into small rounds, fry until puffed and golden, then fill with the preserves using a piping bag or small knife.
Dust with sugar.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, egg, and warm milk into a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead briefly, cover, and rest 25-30 minutes until slightly puffed.
Divide into small balls and fry in the oil, 2-3 minutes per side, until deeply golden and puffed.
Once cool enough to handle, use a small knife or piping tip to inject each with a spoonful of the preserves.
Dust generously with sugar - sufganiyot, jelly-filled donuts, are a beloved Israeli treat especially associated with Hanukkah.
description: Sufganiyot, jam-filled doughnuts, are made specifically for Hanukkah, when fried foods are traditionally eaten.

name: Israeli Tahini Sauce

ingredients.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

ingredients.note: cup, chopped, as a parsley stand-in

steps: Whisk the tahini, lime juice, and garlic together, adding water gradually until smooth and pourable - it will seize before it loosens.
Stir in the chopped herbs.
This tahini sauce is essential to Israeli street food, poured over falafel, sabich, and shawarma alike.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps_detailed: Whisk the tahini, lime juice, and garlic together, adding water gradually until smooth and pourable - it will seize before it loosens.
Stir in the chopped herbs.
This tahini sauce is essential to Israeli street food, poured over falafel, sabich, and shawarma alike.
Real fresh parsley is used here now rather than cilantro standing in for it - the two herbs taste meaningfully different, and this dish specifically calls for parsley's flavor.
description: A simple tahini sauce, the base for many Israeli dips.

name: Israeli Amba (Pickled Mango Sauce)
steps: Mash the mango with the turmeric and lime juice.
Season with salt and let sit 10 minutes before serving with sabich or shawarma.
steps_detailed: Mash the mango with the turmeric, lime juice, and garlic until mostly smooth with some texture.
Season with salt and let sit at least 10-15 minutes.
Amba, a tangy pickled mango condiment brought by Iraqi Jewish immigrants, is now inseparable from Israeli sabich and shawarma.
description: Amba, a tangy pickled mango sauce, an Iraqi-Jewish condiment now a common Israeli street-food topping.
